steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Persistent/recurrent choledocholithiasis or biliary obstruction post-ERCP with stent placement; hepatic abscess or biloma; ongoing cholangitis; stent dysfunction
ensemble.information_gap: The physician does not know the current anatomical status of the biliary tree — specifically whether the ERCP stent is patent, whether there is residual ductal dilation, stones, or a developing hepatic collection — that would explain the persistent elevation of direct bilirubin (4.2 mg/dL), transaminases (ALT 240, AST 179), leukocytosis with left shift (WBC 10.9, ANC 9.6, neutrophils 88.1%), and markedly elevated ferritin (3837 ng/mL) in the post-ERCP period.
ensemble.expected_finding: Biliary ductal dilation (suggesting residual obstruction or stent dysfunction), pericholecystic fluid or gallbladder wall thickening (cholecystitis), or a hepatic fluid collection/abscess; alternatively, a decompressed duct confirming adequate biliary drainage post-stent
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: Given persistent direct hyperbilirubinemia, elevated transaminases, leukocytosis with neutrophilia, markedly elevated ferritin, and ongoing RUQ discomfort after ERCP with stent placement, a right upper quadrant ultrasound is the logical first-line imaging to evaluate biliary anatomy, stent position, ductal caliber, gallbladder status, and any hepatic collections without radiation or contrast risk in a patient with borderline renal function (eGFR 54–66).
grounding: Bilirubin, Direct: 4.2 mg/dL (ref 0.0-0.3)
Bilirubin, Total: 5.1 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)
Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 240.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)
Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 179.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)
White Blood Cells: 10.9 K/uL (ref 4.0-10.0)
Absolute Neutrophil Count: 9.6 K/uL (ref 1.6-6.1)
Neutrophils: 88.1 % (ref 34.0-71.0)
Ferritin: 3837.0 ng/mL (ref 30.0-400.0)
Abd: Soft, mildly tender to palpation on RUQ, negative ___ sign, no rebound, no guarding
History: underwent ERCP on ___ with ___ placement
Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL; eGFR estimated between 54 and 66 mL/min/1.73 m2
ensemble.reasoning: This patient had cholangitis treated with ERCP and biliary stent placement, but continues to have RUQ tenderness, persistent direct hyperbilirubinemia, elevated transaminases, leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance, and a dramatically elevated ferritin — all suggesting ongoing hepatobiliary pathology. No imaging has yet been performed at this institution, so a bedside, non-nephrotoxic ultrasound of the liver and biliary tree is the rational first step to assess ductal caliber (stent patency vs. residual obstruction), gallbladder status (cholecystitis given known cholelithiasis), and any hepatic parenchymal abnormality such as abscess that could explain the inflammatory markers.

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient had cholangitis treated with ERCP and biliary stent placement, but continues to have RUQ tenderness, persistent direct hyperbilirubinemia, elevated transaminases, leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance, and a dramatically elevated ferritin — all suggesting ongoing hepatobiliary pathology. No imaging has yet been performed at this institution, so a bedside, non-nephrotoxic ultrasound of the liver and biliary tree is the rational first step to assess ductal caliber (stent patency vs. residual obstruction), gallbladder status (cholecystitis given known cholelithiasis), and any hepatic parenchymal abnormality such as abscess that could explain the inflammatory markers.

verification.actual_finding: No intrahepatic biliary dilation and a normal common hepatic duct (3 mm), but the gallbladder is distended with stones, sludge, and wall edema suggesting cholecystitis.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The report confirmed one of the expected findings (gallbladder wall thickening/edema with stones consistent with cholecystitis) while also providing the alternative expected finding of a decompressed duct confirming adequate post-stent biliary drainage, together resolving the information gap.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Residual/recurrent choledocholithiasis, biliary stricture, or incomplete biliary drainage post-ERCP with stent placement; ongoing cholangitis
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the biliary tree (common bile duct, intrahepatic ducts) has been adequately drained after ERCP and stent placement, and whether residual stones, stricture, or biliary obstruction persists to explain the ongoing elevated bilirubin (total 5.1, direct 4.2), transaminitis (ALT 240, AST 179), and persistent RUQ discomfort
ensemble.expected_finding: Residual choledocholithiasis, biliary duct dilation, stricture at the level of the CBD or hilum, or confirmation of adequate stent position and biliary decompression; possibly pericholecystic changes consistent with acute cholecystitis given the distended gallbladder with stones, sludge, and wall edema on ultrasound
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has persistent hyperbilirubinemia, transaminitis, RUQ tenderness, and a distended gallbladder with wall edema on ultrasound after ERCP with stent placement for cholangitis, and MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive modality to evaluate the biliary tree anatomy, stent position, residual stones, and ductal pathology that ultrasound cannot adequately characterize.
grounding: History: 'underwent ERCP on ___ with ___ placement' — stent placed for cholangitis
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 5.1 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)' — persistent hyperbilirubinemia post-ERCP
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Direct: 4.2 mg/dL (ref 0.0-0.3)' — predominantly conjugated/obstructive pattern
Labs: 'Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT): 240.0 IU/L' and 'Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 179.0 IU/L' — ongoing hepatocellular/cholestatic injury
Prior imaging: 'The gallbladder is distended, and contains stones and sludge with wall edema' — cholecystitis cannot be excluded
Prior imaging: 'There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation' and 'CHD: 3 mm' — ultrasound shows no obvious dilation but is limited
Prior imaging: 'The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas' — pancreas and distal CBD not assessable on US
Physical examination: 'mildly tender to palpation on RUQ, negative ___ sign, no rebound, no guarding' — persistent RUQ tenderness without peritoneal signs
History: 'blood cultures with GNRs' — prior bacteremia from biliary source
ensemble.reasoning: This patient had cholangitis with bacteremia treated with ERCP and biliary stent placement, yet continues to have persistent direct hyperbilirubinemia (total bili 5.1, direct 4.2), elevated transaminases, RUQ tenderness, and a gallbladder with wall edema on ultrasound — all suggesting incomplete biliary decompression or a new/ongoing biliary process. The ultrasound is limited (pancreas not visualized, distal CBD not assessable), so MRCP is ordered to non-invasively map the entire biliary tree, assess stent position and patency, identify residual choledocholithiasis or stricture, and evaluate the gallbladder more thoroughly to guide the next therapeutic decision (repeat ERCP vs. cholecystectomy).

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Residual/recurrent choledocholithiasis, biliary stricture, or incomplete biliary drainage post-ERCP with stent placement; ongoing cholangitis
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether the biliary tree (common bile duct, intrahepatic ducts) has been adequately drained after ERCP and stent placement, and whether residual stones, stricture, or biliary obstruction persists to explain the ongoing elevated bilirubin (total 5.1, direct 4.2), transaminitis (ALT 240, AST 179), and persistent RUQ discomfort
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Residual choledocholithiasis, biliary duct dilation, stricture at the level of the CBD or hilum, or confirmation of adequate stent position and biliary decompression; possibly pericholecystic changes consistent with acute cholecystitis given the distended gallbladder with stones, sludge, and wall edema on ultrasound
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The patient has persistent hyperbilirubinemia, transaminitis, RUQ tenderness, and a distended gallbladder with wall edema on ultrasound after ERCP with stent placement for cholangitis, and MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive modality to evaluate the biliary tree anatomy, stent position, residual stones, and ductal pathology that ultrasound cannot adequately characterize.

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient had cholangitis with bacteremia treated with ERCP and biliary stent placement, yet continues to have persistent direct hyperbilirubinemia (total bili 5.1, direct 4.2), elevated transaminases, RUQ tenderness, and a gallbladder with wall edema on ultrasound — all suggesting incomplete biliary decompression or a new/ongoing biliary process. The ultrasound is limited (pancreas not visualized, distal CBD not assessable), so MRCP is ordered to non-invasively map the entire biliary tree, assess stent position and patency, identify residual choledocholithiasis or stricture, and evaluate the gallbladder more thoroughly to guide the next therapeutic decision (repeat ERCP vs. cholecystectomy).

verification.actual_finding: The CBD is only mildly prominent at 7 mm and tapers normally toward the ampulla, with only a subtle T2 hypointense signal in the distal CBD on a single sequence (no corroboration on other sequences), mild gallbladder wall thickening with pericholecystic fat stranding but no significant distension, and no definitive residual stones, stricture, or biliary obstruction identified.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The physician expected residual choledocholithiasis, significant biliary dilation, or a clear stricture to explain ongoing cholestatic labs, but MRCP showed no convincing residual stones or obstruction and only borderline CBD prominence, failing to confirm the anticipated biliary pathology.
